A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees and produced penny size hail in Medford.

A funnel cloud was sighted by Skywarn spotters in Woodland Township, but did not touch down. The same parent thunderstorm did knock down several trees in the Browns Mills section of Pemberton Township. It was the second severe thunderstorm to affect Pemberton Township on July 1st.

The gust front from a line of severe thunderstorms knocked down several trees and wires in Pennsauken Township. Public Service Electric and Gas reported about 16,000 of its customers statewide lost power including Camden County. It took most of the night to restore power to all homes and businesses.
Slow moving thunderstorms containing hourly rainfall rates of around 2 inches per hour caused flash flooding of streets in parts of Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding of streets resulted in police rescues of stranded motorists.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms containing hourly rainfall rates of around 2 inches per hour caused flash flooding of streets in parts of Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding of streets resulted in police rescues of stranded motorists on Route 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening line of thunderstorms entered Northeast New Jersey during the afternoon and early evening of the 27th. The storms were still powerful enough to down trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding in the Manalapan Brook basin in southeastern Middlesex County as a Doppler Radar estimated storm totals of between 6 and 8 inches fell over the basin. Damage was estimated at 10.25 million dollars as 308 homes, 25 apartments, 20 businesses and one industrial facility were damaged. Six homes suffered major damage (flooding into the first floor). About 800 persons were evacuated from Jamesburg, Spotswood, Helmetta and Monroe. A state of emergency was declared in Jamesburg. Motorists were trapped in rising flood waters and numerous basements were flooded throughout the county. The dam at Lake Manalapan was opened to prevent the dam from breaking. Utilities cut off the electricity and gas supplies to affected homes. In Jamesburg Borough, the downtown business district was flooded as was the Borough Hall and the Firehouse. About 340 persons were evacuated and some stayed at a Red Cross Shelter at a local school. Most of the downtown businesses were reopened within about one week. Damage in the borough was estimated at 3.4 million dollars. In Spotswood Borough, 360 persons were evacuated from about 120 homes. Damage was estimated at 2.2  million dollars. In Helmetta Borough, it was common to have five feet of water in basements. In Monroe Township, elderly residents from the Rossmoor Adult Community and the Monroe Village Retirement Community were evacuated. Damage was estimated at 1.4 million dollars. Flood waters receded on the 18th and only about 25 homes were without working utilities on the evening of the 18th. The Manalapan River at Spotswood had a a record breaking crest of 20.42 feet on the 20th. The previous record crest was 19.97 feet set in 1989. The period of record for the river gage is 47 years. Storm totals included 3.25 inches in Old Bridge and 1.62 inches in New Brunswick; neither location was in the core of the heaviest rain.
A weakening line of thunderstorms entered Northeast New Jersey during the afternoon and early evening of the 27th. The storms were still powerful enough to down trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines down in Fair Lawn, Wykoff, and Paramus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and very humid air mass enveloped southern New Jersey on the 18th and 19th. High temperatures reached into the lower 90s, but dewpoint temperatures were in the mid 70s. The combination produced afternoon heat indices of between 100 and 105. In addition, the high dewpoints prevented temperatures from falling lower than the 70s in most places at night, especially in urbanized locations. The excessive heat caused the death of two people in Cape May County. The excessive heat caused an increase in the number of elderly people with breathing problems caused by the heat and elevated ozone levels. Hospitals also saw a slight increase in patients suffering heat exhaustion. Atlantic County hospitals treated about 35 persons with heat related illnesses. Camden County opened eight stop-in centers to give the elderly and residents a cool place to go during the extreme heat. The excessive heat put a strain on utilities. Atlantic City Electric set an all-time usage record of 2,683 megawatts between 4 p.m. and 5 p.m. EDT on the 19th. The heat also took its toll on roadways and bridges. The 9th Street Drawbridge could not be closed in Ocean City (Cape May County).A cold front that moved through the state late in the day on the 19th, did not lower temperatures much, but humidity levels were much lower on the 20th through the 24th. Dewpoint temperatures on the afternoon of the 20th were 10 to 15 degrees lower than they were the two previous days. The highest temperatures included 94 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport, 92 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and 91 degrees at the Mercer County Airport.

Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused urban and poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of creeks in southeastern Ocean County from Berkeley Township south through Eagleswood Township. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 5 inches. Most of the rain fell within 90 minutes. The worst reported flooding was in Stafford Township as many roads were flooded. The police issued an advisory for motorists to stay off the road. New Jersey State Route 72 was bumper-to-bumper because of four feet of standing water. Flooding in the Beach Haven West section of Stafford Township caused minor damage to the bulkheads along Manahawkin Bay. Both Mill Creek in Stafford Township and Cedar Creek in Berkeley Township flooded.

Another run of unseasonably hot weather affected New Jersey from August 2nd through the 5th. High temperatures reached at least into the mid 90s each afternoon with some high temperatures around 100 degrees toward New York City.  Hospitals saw an increase in cases of heat exhaustion and respiratory related illnesses. In Mercer County, the United Way donated money to purchase room size air conditioners for medically fragile senior citizens. A weak cold front moved through New Jersey during the night of the 5th and brought temporary relief from the heat and humidity. Highest temperatures included 100 degrees at the Newark International Airport (Essex County), 98 degrees in Somerville (Somerset County), Seabrook (Cumberland County) and Phillipsburg (Warren County), 97 degrees in New Brunswick (Middlesex County), Mount Holly (Burlington County) and Pennsauken (Camden County), 96 degrees in Flemington (Hunterdon County) and at both the Philadelphia International Airport and Atlantic City International Airport and in Trenton (Mercer County), 94 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County) and Sussex (Sussex County), 93 degrees in Belmar (Monmouth County) and 91 degrees in Wildwood (Cape May County).

September 2005 was an unseasonably warm and dry month across the state of New Jersey. The unseasonably dry weather began for most in mid August and continued throughout the month. The lack of rain affected both the agriculture and water supplies. Acting Governor Richard Codey declared a drought watch on September 13th and called for voluntary conservation methods. Residents were urged to limit lawn watering, to only run their washers and dishwashers with full loads and to purchase and install water saving shower heads and faucets. The state's Forestry Service also prohibited the creating of recreational fires that burn directly on the ground and in all wooded areas.  The boroughs of Butler and Kinnelon (Morris County) placed restrictions on all outdoor use of water.Because of the higher cost for fuel, irrigation costs increased. The heat damaged the leaves of temperature sensitive plants such as lettuce, parsley and cilantro. The lack of rain reduced the size of lettuce, cabbage, leeks and arugula. The sun helped cause blistering of tomatoes, peppers, squash and beans. Some tree leaves were showing signs of scorching, especially along their edges. Dogwoods and maples showed signs of water stress. Milk production was down because of the affect the heat had on the cows. As one farmer was quoted, operational costs were up about 20 percent and yields were down 25 percent.Another problem with the drought affected power lines near the coast. The lack of rain permitted salt to build on power lines. When it rained at the end of the month, blown fuses, arcing wires and pole fires occurred. About 9,000 homes and businesses mainly in Atlantic and Cape May Counties lost power.Dating back to 1895, it was the second warmest and fifth driest September on record for the state of New Jersey. Across the state monthly county precipitation averages ranged from 0.2 inches in Cape May County to 2.4 inches in Mercer and Essex Counties. Normal is about 3.8 inches. The southern half of the state was drier than the northern half. The September average monthly temperature of 71.3 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport was the sixth warmest September on record and the 0.53 inches of precipitation was the seventh driest September on record. At the Mercer County Airport near Trenton, the monthly average temperature of 71.2 degrees was 4.2 degrees warmer than normal and the monthly precipitation total of 2.94 inches was 0.48 inches drier than normal.

A severe thunderstorm tore down several large tree limbs and wires in both the city of Camden and the Woodlynne Borough.

Heavy rain associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Cindy fell across New Jersey from early morning into the early afternoon on the 8th. The rain itself started during the late evening of the 7th in the southwest part of the state and overspread the rest of the state well before sunrise on the 8th. The rain ended during the afternoon of the 8th. Storm totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches except for a narrow band of a radar estimated 4 to 5 inches that paralleled the Garden State Parkway in Ocean and Atlantic Counties.    The heavy rain caused poor drainage and roadway flooding, but did not fall in a concentrated burst. Thus no serious stream or river flooding was reported. In Gloucester County in Logan Township, emergency repairs were made to a 30 foot breach in a levee off of Floodgate Road to keep the Delaware River at bay. The combination of the heavy rain and northeast winds gusting around 20 to 30 mph knocked down a few weak trees.Specific storm totals included 3.25 inches in Bayville (Ocean County), 2.68 inches in Point Pleasant (Ocean County), 2.44 inches in Margate (Atlantic County), 2.30 inches in Butler (Morris County), 2.15 inches in Lebanon Township (Hunterdon County), 2.12 inches in South Seaville (Cape May County), 1.88 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County) and Wildwood (Cape May County), 1.85 inches in Willingboro (Burlington County), 1.83 inches in Trenton (Mercer County), 1.74 inches in Stewartsville (Warren County), 1.68 inches in Andover (Sussex County), 1.46 inches in Somerville (Somerset County) and 1.43 inches in Millville (Cumberland County). The remnants of Tropical Storm Cindy moved from near Atlanta, Georgia at 8 a.m. EDT on the 7th northeast to near Washington, D.C. at 8 a.m. EDT on the 8th, lower Delaware at 2 p.m. EDT on the 8th and about 100 miles east of Atlantic City at 8 p.m. EDT on the 8th.

A severe thunderstorm caused considerable tree and wire damage in northwestern Camden County. Several roads were closed because of fallen trees. While several homes and vehicles were damaged by the fallen trees, there were no reports of serious injuries. About 5,200 homes and businesses lost power. The worst damage was centered in Haddon Heights Borough. In Haddon Heights, many mature sycamore trees were knocked down. A few homes and vehicles were damaged along the Black Horse Pike.  On West High Street, several trees fell onto one home. Homes were also damaged by fallen trees on Austermuhl and Crystal Lakes Avenues. Wind damage was not confined to Haddon Heights. In Mount Ephraim Borough, a home was also damaged by downed trees. Haddonfield Borough had to bring in extra crews to clear away the downed trees and limbs. In Audubon Borough, a downed tree blocked a road. In Cherry Hill Township, downed wires closed the eastbound lanes of busy New Jersey State Route 70.

Highest temperatures included 100 degrees at the Newark International Airport (Essex County), 98 degrees in Somerville (Somerset County), Seabrook (Cumberland County) and Phillipsburg (Warren County), 97 degrees in New Brunswick (Middlesex County), Mount Holly (Burlington County) and Pennsauken (Camden County), 96 degrees in Flemington (Hunterdon County) and at both the Philadelphia International Airport and Atlantic City International Airport and in Trenton (Mercer County), 94 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County) and Sussex (Sussex County), 93 degrees in Belmar (Monmouth County) and 91 degrees in Wildwood (Cape May County).

The last prolonged run of excessive heat of the summer matched the late July one for both temperatures and relative humidity levels. High temperatures on the 13th and 14th (the two hottest days) reached well into the 90s and even around 100 toward New York City. Afternoon heat indices those two days reached as high as 105 to 110 degrees Many utilities set weekend usage records (the two hottest days were on Saturday the 13th and Sunday the 14th). Public Service Electric and Gas set a new weekend usage record of 9,425 megawatts. In Somerville (Somerset County), dozens of youths collapsed during a Step Drill Competition at the unairconditioned Somerville Middle School on the 13th. The competition was shut down at 330 p.m. EDT. The high temperature that day in Somerville was 100 degrees. Hospitals saw a slight increase in heat exhaustion cases. Fall athletic practices were curtailed because of the heat. The excessive heat ended as a strong cold front moved through New Jersey during the night of the 14th.  Highest temperatures during the current hot spell included 102 degrees at the Newark International Airport (Essex County), 100 degrees in Somerville (Somerset County), 99 degrees in Belmar (Monmouth County) 98 degrees in Estell Manor (Atlantic County) and Flemington (Hunterdon County), 97 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and Somerdale and Pennsauken (Camden County), 96 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County Airport), Sussex (Sussex County), Atsion (Burlington County), Hammonton (Atlantic County) and at the Atlantic City International Airport, 95 degrees in Mount Holly (Burlington County) and Margate (Atlantic County), 94 degrees in Seabrook (Cumberland County), 92 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County) and 90 degrees in Wildwood (Cape May County).The unseasonably hot start to the first half of August helped cement it as not only the warmest August on record, but also the warmest summer on record in the state since statewide records began in 1895. The preliminary statewide August monthly average temperature was 77.3 degrees and for the summer was 75.6 degrees. The latter shattered the previous summer record of 74.6 degrees set in 1949 by a whole degree. At the Atlantic City International Airport, the August monthly mean temperature of 75.8 degrees and the summer mean temperature of 77.9 degrees were both the warmest on record.

A series of thunderstorms with heavy rain in spite of the dry weather during the past month produced poor drainage flooding and flooding of some of the smaller streams in northwestern Middlesex County and Eastern Somerset County. The heavy rain fell during the evening commute and poor drainage flooding on roadways let to closures and a more difficult than typical evening commute home. The worst reported flooding problems were in North Plainfield Borough in Somerset County and Piscataway Township and Middlesex Borough in Middlesex County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 3 and 4 inches along the county border between Middlesex and Somerset Counties. Actual storm totals included 2.18 inches in Middlesex Borough (Middlesex County), 2.10 inches in North Plainfield (Somerset County), 2.09 inches in Deans (Middlesex County) and 1.35 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County).

A severe thunderstorm tore down trees and power lines in Newton and Byram Township. This was the fourth of five severe thunderstorms to affect Sussex County on this day and the one that produced the longest power outages. Statewide about 100,000 homes and businesses lost power from the severe thunderstorms and lightning during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. In Sussex County, about 1,400 homes and businesses lost power. All power was restored by the 15th with the outages lasting the longest in Byram Township and Newton.

At the Mercer County Airport near Trenton, the monthly average temperature of 71.2 degrees was 4.2 degrees warmer than normal and the monthly precipitation total of 2.94 inches was 0.48 inches drier than normal.

The combination of the spring tides associated with the full moon and the peripheral northeast circulation along the western flanks of Tropical Storm Ophelia caused pockets of minor tidal flooding at the times of the evening high tide on the 16th. The tide reached 6.98 feet above mean lower water water at Cape May (Cape May County) and 6.75 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. Isolated minor tidal flooding occurred at Cape May with the evening high tides on the 15th and 17th.

The tide reached 6.98 feet above mean lower water water at Cape May (Cape May County) and 6.75 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. Isolated minor tidal flooding occurred at Cape May with the evening high tides on the 15th and 17th.

A very moist and highly unstable airmass was in place ahead of a cold front on the 14th. Several rounds of severe thunderstorms developed in this airmass. These thunderstorms produced quite a bit of wind damage which knocked out power for several thousands of residents in Northeast New Jersey. Lightning strikes were also reported, some of which knocked out power and started fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hundreds of trees knocked down in a 2 to 3 square mile area due to a microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms with heavy rain in spite of the dry weather during the past month produced poor drainage flooding and flooding of some of the smaller streams in southeastern Morris County. The heaviest rain fell from East Hanover Township south through Chatham Township. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 2 and 4 inches in this area.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees and wires in Cinnaminson Township.

Lightning struck the chimney of a house in Washington Township. The ensuing fire scorched the vinyl siding and shattered an upper window. A 21-year-old woman sleeping inside the home awoke and escaped without getting injured. It was estimated that the family would be displaced for about four weeks until repairs to the home were completed.

Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding. The heavy rain also caused the evacuation and displacement to Willingboro Township of residents of the Garden State Rest Home after the facility's roof leaked. The rain water damaged the rest home's electrical system. Storm totals included 2.30 inches in Willingboro and 2.03 inches in Mount Laurel.

A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires in Hopatcong Borough. This was the last of five severe thunderstorms to affect Sussex County on this day. Statewide about 100,000 homes and businesses lost power from the severe thunderstorms and lightning during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. In Sussex County, about 1,400 homes and businesses lost power. All power was restored by the 15th.

Some of the highest spring tides of the year due to the perigean full moon caused minor tidal flooding at the times of evening high tide on the 21st and 22nd. More isolated minor tidal flooding occurred during the late afternoon and evenings of the 19th, 20th and 23rd. Tides reached 7.06 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County) the evening of the 22nd and 6.95 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) the evening of the 21st. Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another run of unseasonably hot weather affected New Jersey from August 2nd through the 5th. High temperatures reached at least into the mid 90s each afternoon with some high temperatures around 100 degrees toward New York City.  Hospitals saw an increase in cases of heat exhaustion and respiratory related illnesses. In Mercer County, the United Way donated money to purchase room size air conditioners for medically fragile senior citizens. A weak cold front moved through New Jersey during the night of the 5th and brought temporary relief from the heat and humidity. A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs along Sussex County Route 628 in Wantage Township. This was the first of five severe thunderstorms to affect Sussex County on this day. Statewide about 100,000 homes and businesses lost power from the severe thunderstorms and lightning during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. In Sussex County, about 1,400 homes and businesses lost power. All power was restored by the 15th.

September 2005 was an unseasonably warm and dry month across the state of New Jersey. The unseasonably dry weather began for most in mid August and continued throughout the month. The lack of rain affected both the agriculture and water supplies. Acting Governor Richard Codey declared a drought watch on September 13th and called for voluntary conservation methods. Residents were urged to limit lawn watering, to only run their washers and dishwashers with full loads and to purchase and install water saving shower heads and faucets. The state's Forestry Service also prohibited the creating of recreational fires that burn directly on the ground and in all wooded areas.  The boroughs of Butler and Kinnelon (Morris County) placed restrictions on all outdoor use of water.Because of the higher cost for fuel, irrigation costs increased. The heat damaged the leaves of temperature sensitive plants such as lettuce, parsley and cilantro. The lack of rain reduced the size of lettuce, cabbage, leeks and arugula. The sun helped cause blistering of tomatoes, peppers, squash and beans. Some tree leaves were showing signs of scorching, especially along their edges. Dogwoods and maples showed signs of water stress. Milk production was down because of the affect the heat had on the cows. As one farmer was quoted, operational costs were up about 20 percent and yields were down 25 percent.Another problem with the drought affected power lines near the coast. The lack of rain permitted salt to build on power lines. When it rained at the end of the month, blown fuses, arcing wires and pole fires occurred. About 9,000 homes and businesses mainly in Atlantic and Cape May Counties lost power.Dating back to 1895, it was the second warmest and fifth driest September on record for the state of New Jersey. Across the state monthly county precipitation averages ranged from 0.2 inches in Cape May County to 2.4 inches in Mercer and Essex Counties. Normal is about 3.8 inches. The southern half of the state was drier than the northern half. The September average monthly temperature of 71.3 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport was the sixth warmest September on record and the 0.53 inches of precipitation was the seventh driest September on record. At the Mercer County Airport near Trenton, the monthly average temperature of 71.2 degrees was 4.2 degrees warmer than normal and the monthly precipitation total of 2.94 inches was 0.48 inches drier than normal.

Heavy rain associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Cindy fell across New Jersey from early morning into the early afternoon on the 8th. The rain itself started during the late evening of the 7th in the southwest part of the state and overspread the rest of the state well before sunrise on the 8th. The rain ended during the afternoon of the 8th. Storm totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches except for a narrow band of a radar estimated 4 to 5 inches that paralleled the Garden State Parkway in Ocean and Atlantic Counties.    The heavy rain caused poor drainage and roadway flooding, but did not fall in a concentrated burst. Thus no serious stream or river flooding was reported. In Gloucester County in Logan Township, emergency repairs were made to a 30 foot breach in a levee off of Floodgate Road to keep the Delaware River at bay. The combination of the heavy rain and northeast winds gusting around 20 to 30 mph knocked down a few weak trees.Specific storm totals included 3.25 inches in Bayville (Ocean County), 2.68 inches in Point Pleasant (Ocean County), 2.44 inches in Margate (Atlantic County), 2.30 inches in Butler (Morris County), 2.15 inches in Lebanon Township (Hunterdon County), 2.12 inches in South Seaville (Cape May County), 1.88 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County) and Wildwood (Cape May County), 1.85 inches in Willingboro (Burlington County), 1.83 inches in Trenton (Mercer County), 1.74 inches in Stewartsville (Warren County), 1.68 inches in Andover (Sussex County), 1.46 inches in Somerville (Somerset County) and 1.43 inches in Millville (Cumberland County). The remnants of Tropical Storm Cindy moved from near Atlanta, Georgia at 8 a.m. EDT on the 7th northeast to near Washington, D.C. at 8 a.m. EDT on the 8th, lower Delaware at 2 p.m. EDT on the 8th and about 100 miles east of Atlantic City at 8 p.m. EDT on the 8th.

The gust front from a severe thunderstorm downed trees, tree limbs and and wires in northwestern Burlington County including the townships of Willingboro, Maple Shade and Mount Laurel. At the Riverton Yacht Club, the gust front capsized eleven boats and sixteen people tumbled into the Delaware River. They were rescued without being injured. Public Service Electric and Gas reported about 16,000 of its customers statewide lost power including Burlington County. It took most of the night to restore power to all homes and businesses with homes in Willingboro the last to have their power restored.

Lightning struck a girl that first hit the ground in Stillwater. The girl was taken to the hospital with minor injuries. The girl was in the Girl Scouts, and the counselor was moving campers to a shelter when the incident happened.

Lighting struck the roof of two homes in Parsippany, resulting in a small fire in the rear of one of the homes. The fire was able to be extinguished by the homeowner. One of the homes however could not be re-occupied.

A home was struck by lightning, resulting in a small fire. The fire was quickly extinguished before firefighters arrived. Damage to the home was confined to some siding near the peak at the roof line, and a broken gutter. No internal damage occurred.

Another run of unseasonably hot weather affected New Jersey from August 2nd through the 5th. High temperatures reached at least into the mid 90s each afternoon with some high temperatures around 100 degrees toward New York City.  Hospitals saw an increase in cases of heat exhaustion and respiratory related illnesses. In Mercer County, the United Way donated money to purchase room size air conditioners for medically fragile senior citizens. A weak cold front moved through New Jersey during the night of the 5th and brought temporary relief from the heat and humidity. Highest temperatures included 100 degrees at the Newark International Airport (Essex County), 98 degrees in Somerville (Somerset County), Seabrook (Cumberland County) and Phillipsburg (Warren County), 97 degrees in New Brunswick (Middlesex County), Mount Holly (Burlington County) and Pennsauken (Camden County), 96 degrees in Flemington (Hunterdon County) and at both the Philadelphia International Airport and Atlantic City International Airport and in Trenton (Mercer County), 94 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County) and Sussex (Sussex County), 93 degrees in Belmar (Monmouth County) and 91 degrees in Wildwood (Cape May County).

The last prolonged run of excessive heat of the summer matched the late July one for both temperatures and relative humidity levels. High temperatures on the 13th and 14th (the two hottest days) reached well into the 90s and even around 100 toward New York City. Afternoon heat indices those two days reached as high as 105 to 110 degrees Many utilities set weekend usage records (the two hottest days were on Saturday the 13th and Sunday the 14th). Public Service Electric and Gas set a new weekend usage record of 9,425 megawatts. In Somerville (Somerset County), dozens of youths collapsed during a Step Drill Competition at the unairconditioned Somerville Middle School on the 13th. The competition was shut down at 330 p.m. EDT. The high temperature that day in Somerville was 100 degrees. Hospitals saw a slight increase in heat exhaustion cases. Fall athletic practices were curtailed because of the heat. The excessive heat ended as a strong cold front moved through New Jersey during the night of the 14th.  Highest temperatures during the current hot spell included 102 degrees at the Newark International Airport (Essex County), 100 degrees in Somerville (Somerset County), 99 degrees in Belmar (Monmouth County) 98 degrees in Estell Manor (Atlantic County) and Flemington (Hunterdon County), 97 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and Somerdale and Pennsauken (Camden County), 96 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County Airport), Sussex (Sussex County), Atsion (Burlington County), Hammonton (Atlantic County) and at the Atlantic City International Airport, 95 degrees in Mount Holly (Burlington County) and Margate (Atlantic County), 94 degrees in Seabrook (Cumberland County), 92 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County) and 90 degrees in Wildwood (Cape May County).The unseasonably hot start to the first half of August helped cement it as not only the warmest August on record, but also the warmest summer on record in the state since statewide records began in 1895. The preliminary statewide August monthly average temperature was 77.3 degrees and for the summer was 75.6 degrees. The latter shattered the previous summer record of 74.6 degrees set in 1949 by a whole degree. At the Atlantic City International Airport, the August monthly mean temperature of 75.8 degrees and the summer mean temperature of 77.9 degrees were both the warmest on record.

At the Atlantic City International Airport, the August monthly mean temperature of 75.8 degrees and the summer mean temperature of 77.9 degrees were both the warmest on record.

The last prolonged run of excessive heat of the summer matched the late July one for both temperatures and relative humidity levels. High temperatures on the 13th and 14th (the two hottest days) reached well into the 90s and even around 100 toward New York City. Afternoon heat indices those two days reached as high as 105 to 110 degrees Many utilities set weekend usage records (the two hottest days were on Saturday the 13th and Sunday the 14th). Public Service Electric and Gas set a new weekend usage record of 9,425 megawatts. In Somerville (Somerset County), dozens of youths collapsed during a Step Drill Competition at the unairconditioned Somerville Middle School on the 13th. The competition was shut down at 330 p.m. EDT. The high temperature that day in Somerville was 100 degrees. Hospitals saw a slight increase in heat exhaustion cases. Fall athletic practices were curtailed because of the heat. The excessive heat ended as a strong cold front moved through New Jersey during the night of the 14th. Another run of unseasonably hot weather affected New Jersey from August 2nd through the 5th. High temperatures reached at least into the mid 90s each afternoon with some high temperatures around 100 degrees toward New York City.  Hospitals saw an increase in cases of heat exhaustion and respiratory related illnesses. In Mercer County, the United Way donated money to purchase room size air conditioners for medically fragile senior citizens. A weak cold front moved through New Jersey during the night of the 5th and brought temporary relief from the heat and humidity. A series of thunderstorms with heavy rain in spite of the dry weather during the past month produced poor drainage flooding and flooding of some of the smaller streams in northwestern Middlesex County and Eastern Somerset County. The heavy rain fell during the evening commute and poor drainage flooding on roadways let to closures and a more difficult than typical evening commute home. The worst reported flooding problems were in North Plainfield Borough in Somerset County and Piscataway Township and Middlesex Borough in Middlesex County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 3 and 4 inches along the county border between Middlesex and Somerset Counties. Actual storm totals included 2.18 inches in Middlesex Borough (Middlesex County), 2.10 inches in North Plainfield (Somerset County), 2.09 inches in Deans (Middlesex County) and 1.35 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County).

September 2005 was an unseasonably warm and dry month across the state of New Jersey. The unseasonably dry weather began for most in mid August and continued throughout the month. The lack of rain affected both the agriculture and water supplies. Acting Governor Richard Codey declared a drought watch on September 13th and called for voluntary conservation methods. Residents were urged to limit lawn watering, to only run their washers and dishwashers with full loads and to purchase and install water saving shower heads and faucets. The state's Forestry Service also prohibited the creating of recreational fires that burn directly on the ground and in all wooded areas.  The boroughs of Butler and Kinnelon (Morris County) placed restrictions on all outdoor use of water.Because of the higher cost for fuel, irrigation costs increased. The heat damaged the leaves of temperature sensitive plants such as lettuce, parsley and cilantro. The lack of rain reduced the size of lettuce, cabbage, leeks and arugula. The sun helped cause blistering of tomatoes, peppers, squash and beans. Some tree leaves were showing signs of scorching, especially along their edges. Dogwoods and maples showed signs of water stress. Milk production was down because of the affect the heat had on the cows. As one farmer was quoted, operational costs were up about 20 percent and yields were down 25 percent.Another problem with the drought affected power lines near the coast. The lack of rain permitted salt to build on power lines. When it rained at the end of the month, blown fuses, arcing wires and pole fires occurred. About 9,000 homes and businesses mainly in Atlantic and Cape May Counties lost power.Dating back to 1895, it was the second warmest and fifth driest September on record for the state of New Jersey. Across the state monthly county precipitation averages ranged from 0.2 inches in Cape May County to 2.4 inches in Mercer and Essex Counties. Normal is about 3.8 inches. The southern half of the state was drier than the northern half. The September average monthly temperature of 71.3 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport was the sixth warmest September on record and the 0.53 inches of precipitation was the seventh driest September on record. At the Mercer County Airport near Trenton, the monthly average temperature of 71.2 degrees was 4.2 degrees warmer than normal and the monthly precipitation total of 2.94 inches was 0.48 inches drier than normal.

Runoff from the series of thunderstorms with heavy rain during the day caused minor flooding along the Cooper River during the evening of the 8th. The Cooper River at Haddonfield was above its 2.8 foot flood stage from 814 p.m. through 1034 p.m. EDT on the 8th. It crested at 2.88 feet at 924 p.m. EDT. Doppler Radar storm total estimates averaged 1.5 inches.

Heavy rain associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Cindy fell across New Jersey from early morning into the early afternoon on the 8th. The rain itself started during the late evening of the 7th in the southwest part of the state and overspread the rest of the state well before sunrise on the 8th. The rain ended during the afternoon of the 8th. Storm totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches except for a narrow band of a radar estimated 4 to 5 inches that paralleled the Garden State Parkway in Ocean and Atlantic Counties.    The heavy rain caused poor drainage and roadway flooding, but did not fall in a concentrated burst. Thus no serious stream or river flooding was reported. In Gloucester County in Logan Township, emergency repairs were made to a 30 foot breach in a levee off of Floodgate Road to keep the Delaware River at bay. The combination of the heavy rain and northeast winds gusting around 20 to 30 mph knocked down a few weak trees.Specific storm totals included 3.25 inches in Bayville (Ocean County), 2.68 inches in Point Pleasant (Ocean County), 2.44 inches in Margate (Atlantic County), 2.30 inches in Butler (Morris County), 2.15 inches in Lebanon Township (Hunterdon County), 2.12 inches in South Seaville (Cape May County), 1.88 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County) and Wildwood (Cape May County), 1.85 inches in Willingboro (Burlington County), 1.83 inches in Trenton (Mercer County), 1.74 inches in Stewartsville (Warren County), 1.68 inches in Andover (Sussex County), 1.46 inches in Somerville (Somerset County) and 1.43 inches in Millville (Cumberland County). The remnants of Tropical Storm Cindy moved from near Atlanta, Georgia at 8 a.m. EDT on the 7th northeast to near Washington, D.C. at 8 a.m. EDT on the 8th, lower Delaware at 2 p.m. EDT on the 8th and about 100 miles east of Atlantic City at 8 p.m. EDT on the 8th.

The combination of the spring tides associated with the full moon and the peripheral northeast circulation along the western flanks of Tropical Storm Ophelia caused pockets of minor tidal flooding at the times of the evening high tide on the 16th. The tide reached 6.98 feet above mean lower water water at Cape May (Cape May County) and 6.75 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. Isolated minor tidal flooding occurred at Cape May with the evening high tides on the 15th and 17th.

Repeated thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding and flooding of some of the smaller streams in northwestern Burlington County from Mount Laurel and Burlington Townships east through Medford Lakes Borough. Saint Andrews Drive in the Ramblewood section of Mount Laurel looked like a lake. Specific storm totals included 3.00 inches in Tabernacle and 2.78 inches in Burlington Township.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and power lines in the northwest part of Cumberland County with tree and wire damage reported in the city of Bridgeton, Shiloh Borough and in Hopewell and Stow Creek Townships. About 4,800 homes and businesses lost power. All power was restored by Midnight EDT that night. The severe weather forced the cancellation of an outdoor event in Bridgeton.

The second severe thunderstorm for the day caused wind damage across the central third of Morris County from Roxbury and Randolph Townships eastward through Parsippany. In Parsippany, one downed tree fell into the family pool of one home. The downed trees, tree limbs and wires caused about 18,000 homes and businesses to lose power in northwestern New Jersey from all of the severe storms. The downed tree limbs and wires also caused about one hour delays for westbound New Jersey Commuter Trains between Essex and Morris Counties and 15 minute delays for eastbound New Jersey Commuter Trains between Boonton and Montclair.
A Mount Laurel home was struck by lightning and blew apart its chimney. No additional structural damage reported.
A severe thunderstorm tore down wires and large tree limbs on Brace Road in Cherry Hill Township.

A very moist and highly unstable airmass was in place ahead of a cold front on the 14th. Several rounds of severe thunderstorms developed in this airmass. These thunderstorms produced quite a bit of wind damage which knocked out power for several thousands of residents in Northeast New Jersey. Lightning strikes were also reported, some of which knocked out power and started fires.

Thunderstorms with frequent cloud-to-ground lightning caused two house fires in Morris County. The lightning caused about 8,000 homes and businesses to lose power in Warren, Hunterdon, Sussex and Morris (1,100 without power) Counties in northwestern New Jersey. Power was not fully restored for the last 1,000 until that evening. In Wharton Borough, a large tree was struck by lightning. It snapped and hit the side of a house. The ensuing fire started on a rear exterior wall and was spreading into the second floor of the home. All of the second floor and some of the first floor suffered water damage. In Mercer County, the United Way donated money to purchase room size air conditioners for medically fragile senior citizens. A weak cold front moved through New Jersey during the night of the 5th and brought temporary relief from the heat and humidity. Highest temperatures included 100 degrees at the Newark International Airport (Essex County), 98 degrees in Somerville (Somerset County), Seabrook (Cumberland County) and Phillipsburg (Warren County), 97 degrees in New Brunswick (Middlesex County), Mount Holly (Burlington County) and Pennsauken (Camden County), 96 degrees in Flemington (Hunterdon County) and at both the Philadelphia International Airport and Atlantic City International Airport and in Trenton (Mercer County), 94 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County) and Sussex (Sussex County), 93 degrees in Belmar (Monmouth County) and 91 degrees in Wildwood (Cape May County).

The combination of the spring tides associated with the full moon and the peripheral northeast circulation along the western flanks of Tropical Storm Ophelia caused pockets of minor tidal flooding at the times of the evening high tide on the 16th. The tide reached 6.98 feet above mean lower water water at Cape May (Cape May County) and 6.75 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. Isolated minor tidal flooding occurred at Cape May with the evening high tides on the 15th and 17th.

Gusty northwest winds associated with a cold frontal passage downed a tree near the intersection of Pershing and Pickford Avenues in Ewing Township. The tree tore down power lines which briefly closed those two roads.

Heavy rain associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Cindy fell across New Jersey from early morning into the early afternoon on the 8th. The rain itself started during the late evening of the 7th in the southwest part of the state and overspread the rest of the state well before sunrise on the 8th. The rain ended during the afternoon of the 8th. Storm totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches except for a narrow band of a radar estimated 4 to 5 inches that paralleled the Garden State Parkway in Ocean and Atlantic Counties.    The heavy rain caused poor drainage and roadway flooding, but did not fall in a concentrated burst. Thus no serious stream or river flooding was reported. In Gloucester County in Logan Township, emergency repairs were made to a 30 foot breach in a levee off of Floodgate Road to keep the Delaware River at bay. The combination of the heavy rain and northeast winds gusting around 20 to 30 mph knocked down a few weak trees.Specific storm totals included 3.25 inches in Bayville (Ocean County), 2.68 inches in Point Pleasant (Ocean County), 2.44 inches in Margate (Atlantic County), 2.30 inches in Butler (Morris County), 2.15 inches in Lebanon Township (Hunterdon County), 2.12 inches in South Seaville (Cape May County), 1.88 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County) and Wildwood (Cape May County), 1.85 inches in Willingboro (Burlington County), 1.83 inches in Trenton (Mercer County), 1.74 inches in Stewartsville (Warren County), 1.68 inches in Andover (Sussex County), 1.46 inches in Somerville (Somerset County) and 1.43 inches in Millville (Cumberland County). The remnants of Tropical Storm Cindy moved from near Atlanta, Georgia at 8 a.m. EDT on the 7th northeast to near Washington, D.C. at 8 a.m. EDT on the 8th, lower Delaware at 2 p.m. EDT on the 8th and about 100 miles east of Atlantic City at 8 p.m. EDT on the 8th.

The second severe thunderstorm of the day affected northern Warren County and downed more large tree limbs in Hardwick Township. The greatest reported wind damage with the second storm was in and around Camp No-Be-Bo-Sco on Sand Point Road in the township. About 7,000 homes and businesses lost power from all of the severe thunderstorms and lightning in northwestern New Jersey that afternoon and evening.

A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires in Vernon Township.  The downed large tree limbs and wires caused about 18,000 homes and businesses to lose power in northwestern New Jersey from all of the severe storms.

Rip currents caused by swells from Hurricanes Maria and Nate claimed the life of one man and seriously injured a 62-year-old woman. The 28-year-old male construction worker drowned off Ocean Bay Boulevard in Dover Township's Ocean Beach Shores section on September 8th. He was one of four members of a construction crew that entered the unguarded stretch of ocean after working on an oceanfront home. Three of the men escaped the rip current and reached the beach with the assistance from onlookers. The fourth male was swept away. He was pulled from the water about 245 p.m. EDT on the 8th and never regained consciousness. The previous day, a 62-year-old woman in Seaside Park nearly drowned after being swept away by a rip current. She was seriously injured, but was expected to survive.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Quinton Township.
Penny to quarter size hail fell in Upper Freehold Township.

Thunderstorms with frequent lightning caused power outages across southern Ocean County and northern Atlantic County. Atlantic City Electric reported about 4,500 homes and businesses lost power in the two counties. The outages were concentrated in Galloway Township in Atlantic County and Stafford and Eagleswood Township in Ocean County. All power was restored by 7 p.m. EDT that evening.

A hot and very humid air mass enveloped southern New Jersey on the 18th and 19th. High temperatures reached into the lower 90s, but dewpoint temperatures were in the mid 70s. The combination produced afternoon heat indices of between 100 and 105. In addition, the high dewpoints prevented temperatures from falling lower than the 70s in most places at night, especially in urbanized locations. The excessive heat caused the death of two people in Cape May County. The excessive heat caused an increase in the number of elderly people with breathing problems caused by the heat and elevated ozone levels. Hospitals also saw a slight increase in patients suffering heat exhaustion. Atlantic County hospitals treated about 35 persons with heat related illnesses. Camden County opened eight stop-in centers to give the elderly and residents a cool place to go during the extreme heat. The excessive heat put a strain on utilities. Atlantic City Electric set an all-time usage record of 2,683 megawatts between 4 p.m. and 5 p.m. EDT on the 19th. The heat also took its toll on roadways and bridges. The 9th Street Drawbridge could not be closed in Ocean City (Cape May County).A cold front that moved through the state late in the day on the 19th, did not lower temperatures much, but humidity levels were much lower on the 20th through the 24th. Dewpoint temperatures on the afternoon of the 20th were 10 to 15 degrees lower than they were the two previous days. The highest temperatures included 94 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport, 92 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and 91 degrees at the Mercer County Airport.

The most hottest and humid air mass of the summer so far occupied New Jersey from the 25th through the 27th. This air mass had as its origin the desert southwest in the United States and a ridge of high pressure aloft brought this air mass east with it through the rest of the country. The hottest and most humid day was the 27th. This was the hottest day of the year so far for most places and the highest temperatures seen in many areas since August of 2002. The combination of high temperatures and humidity levels pushed afternoon heat indices to between 105 and 110 degrees in central and southern New Jersey on the 27th. Hospitals saw an increase in the number of patients complaining about dizziness, allergies and breathing difficulties. Elevated ozone levels exacerbated the symptoms. This time it affected more than just the elderly. The PJM Interconnection recorded a world record for electricity usage on the 26th: 135,000 megawatts. The PJM coordinates the movement of electricity in New Jersey and twelve other states from North Carolina to Illinois. On the 27th, PJM lowered voltage to certain customers by five percent due to the stress on the high voltage lines. Locally Public Service Electric and Gas (PSE&G) (10,780 megawatts), Atlantic City Electric (2,740 megawatts), Jersey Central Power and Light (JCP&L) and the Vineland Municipal Electric Utility (Cumberland County) all set new usage records. The extra demand for electricity did cause a strain on the system and about 3,000 JCP&L customers to lose power over the 26th and 27th in Monmouth County.Hospitals saw even bakers and construction workers suffering from heat related symptoms. The New Jersey Farm Bureau encouraged farm owners and field workers to change their schedules and avoid being outdoors or doing strenuous work during the hottest part of the day, wear hats and stay hydrated. The Hunterdon County Office of the Aging made sure all their clients had air conditioning. In Mercer County, the elderly were encouraged to go to air-conditioned libraries. In Burlington County, the elderly were encouraged to go to the air-conditioned malls.  A cold front abruptly ended the hot weather as it moved through the state late in the afternoon and early in the evening on the 27th.  The highest temperatures included 101 degrees at the Newark International Airport (Essex County), 99 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport, Hamilton (Mercer County) and Chatham (Morris County), 98 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport, Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), Millville (Cumberland County), Somerville (Somerset County) and Somerdale (Camden County), 97 degrees in Atsion (Burlington County), Trenton (Mercer County) and New Brunswick (Middlesex County), 96 degrees in Flemington (Hunterdon County) and Boonton (Morris County) and 95 degrees in Cape May (Cape May County) and Wertsville (Hunterdon County).

A line of severe thunderstorms caused pockets of wind damage across the northern tier of Morris County. Several trees and wires were knocked down. One downed tree damaged a house in Jefferson Township. The downed trees, tree limbs and wires caused about 18,000 homes and businesses to lose power in northwestern New Jersey from all of the severe storms. The downed tree limbs and wires also caused about one hour delays for westbound New Jersey Commuter Trains between Essex and Morris Counties and 15 minute delays for eastbound New Jersey Commuter Trains between Boonton and Montclair.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down large tree limbs and wires in Somerville.

"Pulse severe" thunderstorms developed over Northeast New Jersey on the afternoon of the 19th. The airmass was very moist and unstable. The wind profile was conducive to microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed as a result of severe thunderstorm winds. The wind damage extended southward to the town of Closter, in which a microburst likely occurred. Numerous whole trees were downed with even some treetops sheared off. Penny size hail was observed in Northvale as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down trees and wires across the southern tier of Sussex County from Stillwater Township east through Green Township.

A funnel cloud was sighted by Skywarn spotters in Woodland Township, but did not touch down. The same parent thunderstorm did knock down several trees in the Browns Mills section of Pemberton Township. It was the second severe thunderstorm to affect Pemberton Township on July 1st.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down several large tree limbs and wires in Freehold Township.

A 50-year-old male surfer drowned after he became trapped by a rip current in Wildwood Crest. A fellow surfer tried to rescue him. The beach patrol pulled the unconscious male from the water, but he could not be resuscitated.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in western Camden County from the city of Camden east to Bellmawr Borough. The severe thunderstorm caused its worst wind damage along the Delaware River waterfront in and around the Tweeter Center. At the Tweeter Center, the severe thunderstorm tore awnings and tents, tossed cases of glassware, shattered windows and downed about a dozen trees, tossed and destroyed kiosks, crushed some fencing and caused roof damage to an adjacent industrial building.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Pemberton Township.

A hot and very humid air mass enveloped southern New Jersey on the 18th and 19th. High temperatures reached into the lower 90s, but dewpoint temperatures were in the mid 70s. The combination produced afternoon heat indices of between 100 and 105. In addition, the high dewpoints prevented temperatures from falling lower than the 70s in most places at night, especially in urbanized locations. The excessive heat caused the death of two people in Cape May County. The excessive heat caused an increase in the number of elderly people with breathing problems caused by the heat and elevated ozone levels. Hospitals also saw a slight increase in patients suffering heat exhaustion. Atlantic County hospitals treated about 35 persons with heat related illnesses. Camden County opened eight stop-in centers to give the elderly and residents a cool place to go during the extreme heat. The excessive heat put a strain on utilities. Atlantic City Electric set an all-time usage record of 2,683 megawatts between 4 p.m. and 5 p.m. EDT on the 19th. The heat also took its toll on roadways and bridges. The 9th Street Drawbridge could not be closed in Ocean City (Cape May County).A cold front that moved through the state late in the day on the 19th, did not lower temperatures much, but humidity levels were much lower on the 20th through the 24th. Dewpoint temperatures on the afternoon of the 20th were 10 to 15 degrees lower than they were the two previous days. The highest temperatures included 94 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport, 92 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and 91 degrees at the Mercer County Airport.

A funnel cloud was sighted over the city of Burlington in Burlington County, but did not touch down. But, the parent severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in Chesterfield Township. A second separate severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees and large limbs in Maple Shade Township especially on and around Center Avenue.

A funnel cloud was sighted over the city of Burlington in Burlington County, but did not touch down. But, the parent severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in Chesterfield Township. A second separate severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees and large limbs in Maple Shade Township especially on and around Center Avenue.
A severe thunderstorm pulled down a couple of trees and wires in Plumsted Township.

Lightning strikes caused about 2,900 Atlantic City Electric Company customers to lose power for up to three hours in southeastern New Jersey. The greatest concentration of outages was in Upper Deerfield Township in Cumberland County.

A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires in Pennsville Township. Atlantic City Electric reported about 2,500 homes and businesses lost power across the southern tier of New Jersey. Power was not fully restored to all customers until the 28th.

Thunderstorms with frequent lightning caused power outages across southern Ocean County and northern Atlantic County. Atlantic City Electric reported about 4,500 homes and businesses lost power in the two counties. The outages were concentrated in Galloway Township in Atlantic County and Stafford and Eagleswood Township in Ocean County. All power was restored by 7 p.m. EDT that evening.

Gusty northwest winds buffeted New Jersey during the first half of the day on the 10th. The northwest winds were caused by the difference in surface pressure between an intensifying low pressure system moving northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley in Canada and a high pressure system building east from the central Mississippi Valley. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 46 mph at the Cape May/Lewes Ferry Terminal (Cape May County) and 40 mph at the Atlantic City (Atlantic County) Marina.

A strong northwest flow round a deepening low pressure system produced wind gusts to around 40 mph during the day on the 16th. Under normal circumstances, tree damage would be less, but the week of unseasonably wet weather loosened the soil. This was exacerbated by the unseasonably warm weather which kept more leaves on the trees than is normally the case for the middle of October. A 38-year-old woman was killed in Mountain Lakes (Morris County) around Noon EDT when a tree limb weighing more than 100 pounds struck her in the head. A rotted 100 foot tall oak tree fell onto a home in Chatham (Morris County) and went through the roof in one corner of the home. two vehicles on the property were totaled. Public Service Electric and Gas reported about 6,500 homes and businesses lost power throughout the state. All power was restored by Midnight EDT. The peak wind gust at Newark International Airport (Essex County) was 41 mph.

A persistent onshore flow between a blocking high pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes and a series of low pressure systems moving northeast along the old frontal boundary produced strong winds along the coast on the 13th into the morning of the 14th. Wind damage was further exacerbated by the heavy rain which loosen the soil.  Peak wind gusts averaged 35 to 40 mph inland and around 50 mph along the immediate coast. The combination of the wind and heavy rain helped down numerous trees and subsequently power lines. About 1,300 homes and businesses lost power in southern New Jersey. About 12,000 homes and businesses also lost phone service. It took until October 22nd for all phone service to be restored. Farther north in Ocean County, trees fell down in Point Pleasant and Seaside Park. About 6,500 homes and businesses lost power in Ocean and Monmouth Counties. The old frontal boundary that produced the heavy rain on the 8th stalled in the nearby Atlantic. A series of low pressure systems lifted north along this boundary. A high pressure system over nearby Canada and New England helped block any northward or eastward progression of the frontal boundary and the low pressure systems that rode northward along the front until the 14th. The strongest onshore flow between the high pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes and one of the low pressure systems east of the Delmarva Peninsula peaked on the 13th and into the early part of the 14th. The persistent onshore flow (of varying strength) lasted for about a week until a cold frontal passage moved through the region during the evening on the 15th.

The combination of a very slow moving cold front and copious moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Tammy produced periods of heavy rain across Middlesex County during the morning and evening of the 8th. Storm totals averaged four to six inches. The heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding. One road was closed in Plainsboro Township. Runoff from the heavy rain led to flooding along the Green Brook along the Middelsex and Somerset County Line. The Green Brook at Plainfield (Union County) was above its 8 foot flood stage from 115 a.m. EDT through 749 a.m. EDT on the 9th. It crested at 9.12 feet at 545 a.m. EDT. Storm totals included 5.55 inches in Middlesex and 4.23 inches in New Brunswick. The flooding on the 8th and 9th and the subsequent flooding during the upcoming week damaged over 2,700 single family homes, over 640 apartment units and 220 businesses throughout the state of New Jersey. The cold front moved from near Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania at 8 p.m. EDT on the 7th, to the Susquehanna Valley in Pennsylvania at 2 a.m. EDT on the 8th, to the Poconos and Berks County in Pennsylvania and just west of Chesapeake Bay in Maryland at 8 a.m. EDT on the 8th. The cold front stalled as waves of low pressure road northeast along it. At 2 p.m. EDT on the 8th, the cold front just edged into northwestern New Jersey and the Philadelphia northwest suburbs and was still west of Chesapeake Bay in Maryland. At 8 p.m. EDT that evening the cold front barely moved into the Philadelphia New Jersey suburbs and the Delmarva Peninsula. The cold front and waves of low pressure cleared the Delaware and New Jersey coasts between Midnight EDT and 2 a.m. EDT on the 9th. The slow movement and stalling coupled with an unstable air mass and tropical moisture associated with Tammy helped enhance the torrential downpours. The flooding would have been even worse if not for the unseasonably dry weather that preceded this event from the middle of August.

A strong west pressure gradient developed as an intensifying low pressure system moved north across Northern New England as a ridge of high pressure extended south across the Western Great Lakes.  With very wet antecedent conditions and most trees with full leaf canopies, many trees were downed in saturated ground as peak west winds gusted near 45 mph.Law enforcement reported a large tree down in Nutley.

A persistent onshore flow for nearly a week caused higher than normal tides and this culminated with pockets of moderate tidal flooding during the afternoon and evening high tides on the 12th and 13th. Areas of minor tidal flooding persisting through the afternoon and evening high tides on the 14th and 15th. The onshore flow and large wave action caused moderate beach eroison along the shore. Cape May County faired the best of the ocean counties with vertical cuts that averaged between 1 and 4 feet. The worst reported erosion was in Longport, Ocean City, Strathmere and Avalon. Tidal flooding did close roads in Ocean City. In Atlantic County, vertical cuts averaged 3 to 4 feet. Significant damage to dunes occurred in Atlantic City. Mainly minor tidal flooding was reported. Some streets were closed in Somers Point.  Erosion and tidal flooding was worst in Ocean County. Emergency repairs to dunes was necessary in Beach Haven near Leeward and Merrivale Avenues. Some homes would be endangered without dune repair. Flooding occurred along the Route 72 causeway entrance in Ship Bottom. In Barnegat Bay, tidal flooding affected Waretown (Ocean Township) and Barnegat (Barnegat Township). In Stafford Township, flooding occurred on Cedar Bonnet Island, Mallard Island and Mud City. Two to three foot bayside waves flooded marshes and low-lying areas. Along the oceanfront, widespread vertical cuts of 3 to 4 feet occurred and reached 4 to 6 feet from Barnegat Light south through Surf City. In Ortley Beach, staircases leading to the beach have been ripped away. Beach eriosion down to the base of dunes was reported in Bay Head and Mantloking Boroughs. In Monmouth County, most vertical cuts averaged 2 to 3 feet, but reached up to 4 feet in Belmar and Spring Lake. In Raritan Bay, tidal flooding reached into yards in the Port Monmouth and Leonardo Sections of Middletown Township. Windblown sand was reported in Belmar, Spring Lake and Sea Girt. The tidal flooding helped exacerbate the inland flooding in Monmouth County by slowing the release of water into the ocean.Highest reported tides (all above mean lower low water) included 7.60 feet in North Wildwood (Cape May County), 7.57 feet at Cape May (Cape May County), 7.46 feet at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and 6.81 feet at Atlantic City (Atlantic County).  Minor tidal flooding begins at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water and moderate tidal flooding begins at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water.The old frontal boundary that produced the heavy rain on the 8th stalled in the nearby Atlantic. A series of low pressure systems lifted north along this boundary. A high pressure system over nearby Canada and New England helped block any northward or eastward progression of the frontal boundary and the low pressure systems that rode northward along the front until the 14th. The strongest onshore flow between the high pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes and one of the low pressure systems east of the Delmarva Peninsula occurred on the 13th. The persistent onshore flow (of varying strength) lasted for about a week until a cold frontal passage moved through the region during the evening on the 15th.

In spite of the very heavy rain, there was no flooding along the Delaware River. The South Branch of the Raritan River at High Bridge was above its 10 foot flood stage from 5 a.m. EDT through 845 a.m. EDT on the 9th. It crested at 10.11 feet at 6 a.m. EDT. Farther downstream at Stanton, the South Branch of the Raritan River was above its 6 foot flood stage from 1207 a.m. EDT through 441 a.m. EDT on the 9th. It crested at 8.51 feet at 246 a.m. EDT. Storm totals included 9.07 inches in Califon, 7.18 inches in Flemington, 6.80 inches in Clinton, 6.68 inches in Stockton and 5.88 inches in Wertsville. The flooding on the 8th and 9th and the subsequent flooding during the upcoming week damaged over 2,700 single family homes, over 640 apartment units and 220 businesses throughout the state of New Jersey. The cold front moved from near Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania at 8 p.m. EDT on the 7th, to the Susquehanna Valley in Pennsylvania at 2 a.m. EDT on the 8th, to the Poconos and Berks County in Pennsylvania and just west of Chesapeake Bay in Maryland at 8 a.m. EDT on the 8th. The cold front stalled as waves of low pressure road northeast along it. At 2 p.m. EDT on the 8th, the cold front just edged into northwestern New Jersey and the Philadelphia northwest suburbs and was still west of Chesapeake Bay in Maryland. At 8 p.m. EDT that evening the cold front barely moved into the Philadelphia New Jersey suburbs and the Delmarva Peninsula. The cold front and waves of low pressure cleared the Delaware and New Jersey coasts between Midnight EDT and 2 a.m. EDT on the 9th. The slow movement and stalling coupled with an unstable air mass and tropical moisture associated with Tammy helped enhance the torrential downpours. The flooding would have been even worse if not for the unseasonably dry weather that preceded this event from the middle of August.

The combination of snow melt and the precipitation that fell from the late afternoon of the 15th into the morning of the 16th caused minor flooding along the Rockaway River. The Rockaway River below the Boonton Reservoir was above its 5 foot flood stage from 1135 p.m. EST on the 16th through 1137 a.m. EST on the 17th. It crested at 5.18 feet at 415 a.m. EST on the 17th. Precipitation totals included 1.71 inches in Chatham. The precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that developed on the Gulf Coast early on the 15th. A broad southerly flow brought moisture into the region. The low itself moved northeast through the Delaware Valley overnight on the 15th and was located near New York City at sunrise on the 16th. The track helped bring moisture from both the Atlantic Ocean and the Gulf of Mexico and dropped it across Morris County.

The northwest flow around an intensifying northeaster brought strong and in a few instances high winds to New Jersey during the late morning and early afternoon on the 22nd. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph inland and 50 to 60 mph along the ocean and southern parts of Delaware Bay. Downed tree limbs in East Brunswick (Middlesex County) caused outages to around 500 homes and caused travel disruptions because of non-working street lights. Peak wind gusts included 60 mph at the Cape May Ferry Terminal (Cape May County), 55 mph at the Cape May Coast Guard station (Cape May County), 53 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 52 mph in Brant Beach (Ocean County), 48 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 47 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 44 mph in Atlantic City (Atlantic County) and 37 mph in Trenton (Mercer County). The low pressure system responsible for the strong winds intensified from a 1007mb low near Jacksonville, Florida at 7 a.m. EST on the 21st, to a 996mb low near Wilmington, North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST on the 21st, to a 979mb low about 100 miles east of Atlantic City, New Jersey at 7 a.m. EST on the 22nd, to a 972mb low near Boston, Massachusetts at 1 p.m. EST on the 22nd to a 968mb low in northeastern Maine at 7p.m. EST on the 22nd. The strongest winds occurred as the intensifying low pressure system moved through southern New England.

Heavy rain associated with a low pressure system southeast of New Jersey fell across Burlington County during the evening and overnight on the 13th. The subsequent run-off caused flooding in the Rancocas Basin. Three day storm totals (from the 11th through the 14th)  in the county averaged between three and six inches, although more than half of the heavy rain fell during the night of the 13th. Around 20 families in Eastampton and Lumberton Townships evacuated. In Eastampton Township, flooding along Rabbit Run and the North Branch of the Rancocas Creek cause evacuations from three blocks in the Ewansville Section of the township. Several dozen homes were surrounded by flood waters. In Lumberton Township, two blocks of the downtown section were evacuated. In Southampton Township, minor flooding occurred along the South Branch of the Rancocas Creek in the Vincentown Village. The Trinity Episcopal Church barely avoided being flooded again. In Pemberton Township, two roads were closed near the North Branch of the Rancocas Creek.  The flooding on the 8th and 9th and the flooding during this week damaged over 2,700 single family homes, over 640 apartment units and 220 businesses throughout the state of New Jersey.  The North Branch of the Rancocas Creek at Pemberton was above its 2.5 foot flood stage from 11 a.m. EDT on the 14th through 1245 p.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 2.8 feet at 615 a.m. EDT on the 15th. Specific three day storm totals included 6.08 inches in New Lisbon, 5.38 inches in Lumberton, 4.20 inches in Mount Holly, 3.67 inches in westampton and 3.01 inches at the McGuire Air Force Base.The old frontal boundary that produced the heavy rain on the 8th stalled in the nearby Atlantic. A series of low pressure systems lifted north along this boundary. Moisture from dissipated tropical depression number 22 was ingested into this system from near Bermuda. A high pressure system over nearby Canada and New England helped block any northward or eastward progression of the frontal boundary and the low pressure systems until the 14th. The heavy rain the past week cemented October 2005 as being the wettest October and month on record for the state of New Jersey since state records began in 1895. The preliminary statewide average precipitation total of 11.91 inches was 8.40 inches wetter than normal and would also be the wettest month on record surpassing the August 1955 statewide average of 11.44 inches. October 2005 was also the wettest October on record at Atlantic City International Airport with a precipitation total of 9.04 inches.The October precipitation total in Trenton was 9.92 inches, 7.30 inches wetter than normal.

Periods of heavy rain associated with a series of low pressure systems southeast of New Jersey fell across Ocean County (particularly the northern half) on the 12th and 13th. This caused considerable poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of creeks and rivers as well as one lake.  Three day storm totals ranged from around one inch in the southern part of the county to around ten inches in the northern part of the county. The Metedeconk River flooded in Brick and Lakewood Townships. New Jersey State Route 88 was closed in Lakewood Township. Lake Carasaljio flooded nearby streets in the township. Roads were closed in Dover and Brick Townships. In Jackson Township, a small stream flooded the night of the 13th. Basements and yards were flooded along East Veterans Highway. The flooding on the 8th and 9th and the flooding during this week damaged over 2,700 single family homes, over 640 apartment units and 220 businesses throughout the state of New Jersey.  The North Branch of the Metedeconk River at Lakewood was above its 7 foot flood stage from 830 p.m. EDT on the 12th through 1054 a.m. EDT on the 15th. It crested at 8.58 feet at 1015 p.m. EDT on the 13th. Specific three day storm totals included 11.41 inches in Point Pleasant, 3.10 inches in Barengat Light and 1.14 inches in Brant Beach. The old frontal boundary that produced the heavy rain on the 8th stalled in the nearby Atlantic. A series of low pressure systems lifted north along this boundary. Moisture from dissipated tropical depression number 22 was ingested into this system from near Bermuda. A high pressure system over nearby Canada and New England helped block any northward or eastward progression of the frontal boundary and the low pressure systems until the 14th. The heavy rain the past week cemented October 2005 as being the wettest October and month on record for the state of New Jersey since state records began in 1895. The preliminary statewide average precipitation total of 11.91 inches was 8.40 inches wetter than normal and would also be the wettest month on record surpassing the August 1955 statewide average of 11.44 inches. October 2005 was also the wettest October on record at Atlantic City International Airport with a precipitation total of 9.04 inches.The October precipitation total in Trenton was 9.92 inches, 7.30 inches wetter than normal.

A persistent onshore flow for nearly a week caused higher than normal tides and this culminated with pockets of moderate tidal flooding during the afternoon and evening high tides on the 12th and 13th. Areas of minor tidal flooding persisting through the afternoon and evening high tides on the 14th and 15th. The onshore flow and large wave action caused moderate beach eroison along the shore. Cape May County faired the best of the ocean counties with vertical cuts that averaged between 1 and 4 feet. The worst reported erosion was in Longport, Ocean City, Strathmere and Avalon. Tidal flooding did close roads in Ocean City. In Atlantic County, vertical cuts averaged 3 to 4 feet. Significant damage to dunes occurred in Atlantic City. Mainly minor tidal flooding was reported. Some streets were closed in Somers Point.  Erosion and tidal flooding was worst in Ocean County. Emergency repairs to dunes was necessary in Beach Haven near Leeward and Merrivale Avenues. Some homes would be endangered without dune repair. Flooding occurred along the Route 72 causeway entrance in Ship Bottom. In Barnegat Bay, tidal flooding affected Waretown (Ocean Township) and Barnegat (Barnegat Township). In Stafford Township, flooding occurred on Cedar Bonnet Island, Mallard Island and Mud City. Two to three foot bayside waves flooded marshes and low-lying areas. Along the oceanfront, widespread vertical cuts of 3 to 4 feet occurred and reached 4 to 6 feet from Barnegat Light south through Surf City. In Ortley Beach, staircases leading to the beach have been ripped away. Beach eriosion down to the base of dunes was reported in Bay Head and Mantloking Boroughs. In Monmouth County, most vertical cuts averaged 2 to 3 feet, but reached up to 4 feet in Belmar and Spring Lake. In Raritan Bay, tidal flooding reached into yards in the Port Monmouth and Leonardo Sections of Middletown Township. Windblown sand was reported in Belmar, Spring Lake and Sea Girt. The tidal flooding helped exacerbate the inland flooding in Monmouth County by slowing the release of water into the ocean.Highest reported tides (all above mean lower low water) included 7.60 feet in North Wildwood (Cape May County), 7.57 feet at Cape May (Cape May County), 7.46 feet at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and 6.81 feet at Atlantic City (Atlantic County).  Minor tidal flooding begins at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water and moderate tidal flooding begins at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water.The old frontal boundary that produced the heavy rain on the 8th stalled in the nearby Atlantic. A series of low pressure systems lifted north along this boundary. A high pressure system over nearby Canada and New England helped block any northward or eastward progression of the frontal boundary and the low pressure systems that rode northward along the front until the 14th. The strongest onshore flow between the high pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes and one of the low pressure systems east of the Delmarva Peninsula occurred on the 13th. The persistent onshore flow (of varying strength) lasted for about a week until a cold frontal passage moved through the region during the evening on the 15th.

Gusty northwest winds buffeted New Jersey during the first half of the day on the 10th. The northwest winds were caused by the difference in surface pressure between an intensifying low pressure system moving northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley in Canada and a high pressure system building east from the central Mississippi Valley. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 46 mph at the Cape May/Lewes Ferry Terminal (Cape May County) and 40 mph at the Atlantic City (Atlantic County) Marina.
